body:
# Debug TypeScript Errors

A Claude Code skill that recognizes the 30 most common TypeScript errors and knows the fix for each. Uses progressive disclosure so the base skill stays under 800 tokens.

## Install

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/debug-typescript/references
```

## SKILL.md

```markdown
---
name: debug-typescript
description: >
  Use when hitting a TypeScript compiler error. Triggers on "TS2322",
  "TS2345", "TS2339", "Type 'X' is not assignable", "Argument of type",
  "Cannot find module", "Property 'X' does not exist on type", "Expected N
  argument(s), got M", "overload", "has no overlap", "incompatible with
  signature". Covers: type narrowing, generic constraints, variance, discriminated
  unions, module resolution. Skip for pure runtime errors or conceptual TS questions.
---

# Debug TypeScript

TypeScript errors fall into recognizable shapes. This skill maps common error codes to their cause and fix.

## The 4-phase debug process

### Phase 1 — Identify the error shape

Read the error code (e.g. TS2322) and the first line of the error message. That's your hash.

Match it against `references/error-catalog.md`, which lists the 30 most common codes with cause + fix.

### Phase 2 — Apply the narrow fix

80% of errors have a one-line fix once you know what the error means. Try that first.

### Phase 3 — If the narrow fix fails, read context

Read `references/type-narrowing.md` for type narrowing patterns — probably the most common cause of head-scratching errors.

Read `references/generics.md` for generic constraint errors.

### Phase 4 — If nothing works, minimize

Reproduce the error in a 20-line playground file. If you can't reproduce, the error is from somewhere else in your code.

## The top 10 errors (full matrix in references/error-catalog.md)

| Code | Message | Most common fix |
|---|---|---|
| TS2322 | Type 'X' is not assignable to type 'Y' | Narrow X, or widen Y, or use a type guard |
| TS2339 | Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Y' | Narrow before accessing: `if ('X' in y)` |
| TS2345 | Argument of type 'X' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Y' | Same as TS2322 but for function args |
| TS2307 | Cannot find module 'X' | Install the package OR add types OR check tsconfig paths |
| TS2554 | Expected N arguments, but got M | Function signature mismatch — check the call |
| TS2571 | Object is of type 'unknown' | Type guard or type assertion |
| TS2531 | Object is possibly 'null' | Add null check or use ?. |
| TS2532 | Object is possibly 'undefined' | Same — add the check |
| TS2769 | No overload matches this call | Check which overloads exist, narrow your args |
| TS2739 | Type 'X' is missing properties: Y, Z | Provide the missing fields |

## Anti-patterns this skill rejects

- `as any` — almost always wrong. Use `unknown` + a type guard.
- `// @ts-ignore` — masks the real bug. Use `// @ts-expect-error` with a comment if you must ignore.
- Skipping types via `// @ts-nocheck` on a file — hides everything downstream.
- `as X` where X is unrelated to the value's actual type — that's lying to the compiler.
```

## references/error-catalog.md (excerpt)

```md
# TypeScript Error Catalog

Full matrix of the 30 most common errors, ordered by frequency.

## TS2322 — Type 'X' is not assignable to type 'Y'

The most common error. You have a value of one type and are assigning it to a variable expecting another.

### Cause 1: string vs literal union

```ts
type Status = 'idle' | 'loading' | 'done';
const s: Status = someString; // TS2322 — someString is `string`, not literal union
```

Fix: narrow the string with a type guard:
```ts
if (someString === 'idle' || someString === 'loading' || someString === 'done') {
  const s: Status = someString; // ok
}
```

Or use a parser:
```ts
const s = someString as Status; // risky but occasionally necessary
```

### Cause 2: missing property in an object

```ts
type User = { name: string; email: string };
const u: User = { name: 'Jane' }; // TS2322 — missing 'email'
```

Fix: provide the property.

### Cause 3: extra property via excess property check

```ts
type User = { name: string };
const u: User = { name: 'Jane', age: 30 }; // TS2322 — age is an excess property
```

Fix: remove the extra OR expand the type OR assign via an intermediate variable (which relaxes the check):
```ts
const data = { name: 'Jane', age: 30 };
const u: User = data; // ok — excess check only on literals
```

## TS2339 — Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Y'

[... continues for 28 more codes ...]
```

## references/type-narrowing.md

```md
# Type Narrowing

TypeScript narrows types based on control flow. Understanding the narrow surface is half of debugging.

## typeof

```ts
function f(x: string | number) {
  if (typeof x === 'string') {
    x.toUpperCase(); // x is string here
  }
}
```
